---
title: "Background"
---

This site is a thin layer over data curated by the
[Journal Checker Tool](https://journalcheckertool.org/) (JCT). It helps to know
how that underlying data is assembled, because the linkage between an agreement,
the institutions that take part in it, and the journals it covers is the product
of a voluntary, partly manual, curated process — not an authoritative feed from
publishers.

## The chain of provenance

1. A publisher and a library consortium (or a single institution) negotiate a
   transformative agreement.
2. A consortium or institution **voluntarily** registers the agreement in the
   [ESAC Transformative Agreement Registry](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/agreement-registry/),
   a community-curated registry of agreements between publishers and academic
   institutions. Sharing the terms is
   [entirely voluntary](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/share/),
   and consortium-level agreements are registered at the consortium level rather
   than by each member institution.
3. The JCT data-management team
   [crawls the ESAC registry weekly](https://www.coalition-s.org/blog/enabling-accurate-results-within-the-journal-checker-tool/).
   When it finds a new agreement, it reaches out to the consortium to obtain the
   list of titles covered and which institutions have access, then publishes the
   result in the JCT within about ten working days.
4. The JCT exposes this as CC0
   ["Transformative Agreements Public Data"](https://journalcheckertool.org/transformative-agreements/)
   and an [API](https://journalcheckertool.org/apidocs).
5. This site consumes that public data and matches agreements to TU9
   universities by ROR identifier.

## How the JCT curates it

The crucial step is the third one. ESAC alone does not yield a clean list of
named participating institutions: registration is at the consortium level, and
contributors submit
whatever level of detail they are comfortable providing.
The participating-institution list is therefore obtained by the JCT team
contacting consortia directly and curating the result by hand — it is not
scraped from publishers.

There is also
[no fixed one-to-one relationship](https://www.coalition-s.org/blog/introducing-new-search-features-in-the-journal-checker-tool-for-transformative-agreements/)
between an agreement and its journal and institution lists: a single agreement
can give different institutions access to different sets of titles, which the
JCT records in its own per-agreement sheets.

Maintenance is a distributed partnership. The JCT does not own most of the
underlying data; it draws on
[several sources](https://journalcheckertool.org/data-sources/) — ESAC for the
agreements, plus ROR, Crossref and [DOAJ](https://doaj.org/) for identifiers and
journal data — each updated at the source. Corrections to the agreement index go through the JCT helpdesk,
while portfolio-level changes are submitted back to the original data source.

## What the dates and identifiers mean

Because curation happens in periodic review cycles rather than as a live feed,
the dates in the data are **observational**. Upstream, the JCT records
when it first and last observed
a journal or institution to be part of an agreement — not the actual date it
joined or left, which the JCT does not have. The agreement-level date this site
carries, `last_reviewed`, is the same kind of figure: it marks when the JCT last
reviewed the agreement, not when its journal or institution list last changed.

Journals are identified by their print and online ISSNs, and institutions by
[ROR identifier](https://ror.org/) where available — the same ROR linkage this
site relies on to match an agreement to a TU9 university.

## Caveats

The coverage figures on this site inherit the limits of their source.
Completeness depends on consortia choosing to register their agreements and to
respond to the JCT's requests, and some
[register without disclosing every detail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18255):
the public data dump is a voluntary effort built on the contracts consortia have
publicly disclosed, crowd-sourced from agreement documents and consortia (Jahn
2025). Only fully reviewed agreements that meet the
[ESAC compliance guidelines](https://journalcheckertool.org/faq/) appear in the
public index, and an expired agreement is dropped about three months after it
ends — so the data is a curated snapshot, not a complete or historical record.
Independent validation is nonetheless encouraging: when an open method built on
the JCT and OpenAlex was checked against real publisher data,
[89% of the agreement matches it made were correct](https://doi.org/10.1162/QSS.a.24)
(precision; 2,054 of 2,304), though it missed about half of the true cases (53%
recall) (de Jonge, Kramer & Sondervan 2025). Treat the numbers here as a precise
but conservative lower bound on TU9 participation, not a guarantee of every title
or institution covered.
